questions.q: M. S. Swaminathan is widely known as the father of which movement in India?
questions.explanation: Swaminathan is called the Father of the Green Revolution in India for his role in boosting crop yields and food production.

questions.q: What was M. S. Swaminathan's primary field of science?
questions.explanation: Swaminathan was an agricultural scientist specializing in plant genetics and crop improvement.

questions.q: Swaminathan's work was most famous for dramatically increasing the yield of which crop in India?
questions.explanation: He helped introduce and adapt high-yielding wheat varieties that transformed Indian agriculture in the 1960s.

questions.q: The Green Revolution helped India achieve what major outcome?
questions.explanation: By boosting grain production, the Green Revolution helped India move from food shortages toward self-sufficiency.

questions.q: In which country was M. S. Swaminathan born?
questions.explanation: Swaminathan was born in India, in Kumbakonam in the Madras Presidency (present-day Tamil Nadu).

questions.q: Besides wheat, Swaminathan's Green Revolution efforts also greatly improved the yield of which staple grain?
questions.explanation: High-yielding varieties of rice were also central to the Green Revolution that Swaminathan led.

questions.q: What type of plant trait did Swaminathan focus on developing to increase food production?
questions.explanation: He promoted high-yielding crop varieties that produced much more grain per acre than traditional types.

questions.q: Before the Green Revolution, India frequently struggled with which serious problem that Swaminathan helped address?
questions.explanation: India faced recurring famine and food shortages, which the Green Revolution's higher yields helped overcome.

questions.q: Swaminathan worked to combine modern plant genetics with which goal?
questions.explanation: He applied genetics and breeding to develop crops that produced far higher yields for farmers.

questions.q: M. S. Swaminathan is best described as a champion of which group of people?
questions.explanation: Throughout his career he advocated for farmers' welfare and sustainable, productive agriculture.

questions.q: In which decade did the Green Revolution take hold in India under Swaminathan's leadership?
questions.explanation: The Green Revolution gathered momentum in India during the mid-to-late 1960s with high-yielding wheat varieties.

questions.q: Swaminathan collaborated with which American agronomist, often called the father of the global Green Revolution?
questions.explanation: Swaminathan worked with Norman Borlaug, whose dwarf wheat varieties were adapted for Indian conditions.

questions.q: The high-yielding wheat varieties brought to India during the Green Revolution were notable for being what?
questions.explanation: Semi-dwarf wheat varieties resisted lodging and supported heavy grain heads, greatly raising yields.

questions.q: In which Indian state was M. S. Swaminathan born in 1925?
questions.explanation: He was born in Kumbakonam, in present-day Tamil Nadu, in 1925.

questions.q: Swaminathan founded a research foundation in Chennai focused on sustainable agriculture and rural development. What is it called?
options: The Tata Institute of Fundamental Research
The M. S. Swaminathan Research Foundation
questions.explanation: He established the M. S. Swaminathan Research Foundation (MSSRF) in Chennai in 1988.

questions.q: Swaminathan served as Director General of which major international rice research body?
questions.explanation: He was Director General of the International Rice Research Institute (IRRI) in the Philippines.

questions.q: Swaminathan was the first recipient of which prestigious global food prize in 1987?
questions.explanation: He won the inaugural World Food Prize in 1987 for his contributions to increasing food production.

questions.q: Which of India's highest civilian honours was conferred upon M. S. Swaminathan?
questions.explanation: Swaminathan was awarded the Bharat Ratna, India's highest civilian honour (announced in 2024).

questions.q: Swaminathan headed a government commission in the 2000s that became famous for recommendations on which group?
questions.explanation: He chaired the National Commission on Farmers, whose reports addressed farmer welfare and minimum support prices.

questions.q: In his later career, Swaminathan promoted a concept of agriculture that balances productivity with environmental care. What did he call it?
questions.explanation: He coined the term Evergreen Revolution to describe sustainable, ecologically sound productivity gains.

questions.q: Swaminathan trained in plant genetics and earned his doctorate from which prestigious British university?
questions.explanation: He earned his PhD in genetics from the University of Cambridge in 1952.

questions.q: The TIME magazine recognition of Swaminathan placed him among the most influential of which group in the 20th century?
questions.explanation: TIME magazine named him one of the 20 most influential Asians of the 20th century.

questions.q: Swaminathan worked closely with which Indian official, the agriculture minister credited as a political architect of the Green Revolution?
questions.explanation: Agriculture Minister C. Subramaniam championed the policies that enabled the Green Revolution alongside Swaminathan's scientific work.

questions.q: The Mexican dwarf wheat varieties adapted for India came largely from research at which international center co-founded with Borlaug's work?
questions.explanation: The semi-dwarf wheat varieties originated at CIMMYT (the International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center) in Mexico.

questions.q: In which year did M. S. Swaminathan pass away?
questions.explanation: Swaminathan died in September 2023 at the age of 98.

questions.q: A key recommendation often associated with the Swaminathan Commission was that minimum support prices should be set at what level relative to the cost of production?
questions.explanation: The commission recommended that the minimum support price be at least 50 percent above the weighted average cost of production.

questions.q: Swaminathan held an environment-focused international chair, becoming UNESCO Cousteau Professor in which field?
questions.explanation: He held the UNESCO Cousteau Chair in Ecotechnology, reflecting his focus on linking ecology and technology.

questions.q: Early in his career, Swaminathan declined a position in which field to pursue agricultural science, motivated partly by the Bengal famine?
questions.explanation: Though qualified to study medicine, Swaminathan chose agricultural science, deeply affected by the Bengal famine of 1943.

questions.q: In 1991 Swaminathan received which international environmental award, named after the couple who endowed it?
questions.explanation: He received the Tyler Prize for Environmental Achievement in 1991, one of many honours recognizing his sustainable-agriculture work.

questions.q: Swaminathan's research career in India was closely tied to which major national agricultural research institute in New Delhi?
options: The Saha Institute of Nuclear Physics
questions.explanation: He conducted and led much of his work at the Indian Agricultural Research Institute (IARI), where he later served as Director.
